description: Remnant of a supernova observed in 1054 AD. Filamentary structure visible in long exposures.
imaging_notes: H-alpha filter reveals structure. Requires good seeing for detail.

description: One of the largest and richest globular clusters. Rich core.
imaging_notes: Resolves well at moderate focal lengths.

description: One of the finest globular clusters in the northern sky. Contains ~500,000 stars.
imaging_notes: Bright and well-resolved. Good at most focal lengths.

description: Nearest globular cluster to Earth at ~7,200 light-years. Low elevation from mid-latitudes.
imaging_notes: Low declination challenging from Northern Europe.

description: One of the oldest and largest globular clusters. Estimated age ~13 billion years.
imaging_notes: Beautiful at most focal lengths, well-resolved core.

description: Butterfly-shaped open cluster. Best seen with low magnification.
imaging_notes: Short focal length works best to capture entire cluster.

description: Brilliant naked-eye open cluster. Known since antiquity.
imaging_notes: Very large – use a wide-field setup. Low altitude from Northern Europe.

description: Large emission nebula with embedded open cluster NGC 6530. One of the brightest HII regions.
imaging_notes: H-alpha filter strongly recommended. Pairs well with M20 in same frame.

description: Compact globular cluster in Ophiuchus. Partially obscured by dust.
imaging_notes: Medium difficulty. Background nebulosity adds interest.

description: Bright globular with loose, well-resolved outer regions.
imaging_notes: Pairs well with M12 – only 3° apart in the sky.

description: One of the richest and most compact open clusters. Contains ~2900 stars.
imaging_notes: Bright and photogenic. Short to medium focal length ideal.

description: Loose globular cluster with a less-concentrated core than most.
imaging_notes: Pairs well with M10. Loose structure resolves easily.

description: The finest globular cluster in the northern sky. Visible to the naked eye under dark skies.
imaging_notes: Classic target. Short to medium focal length to capture outer halo.

description: Oval-shaped globular with loosely concentrated core.
imaging_notes: Moderate difficulty. Rich star field.

description: One of the densest globular clusters known. Contains a planetary nebula (Pease 1).
imaging_notes: Very condensed core. Long focal length to resolve outer stars.

description: Famous for the 'Pillars of Creation'. Active star-forming region with embedded cluster.
imaging_notes: Narrowband (H-alpha/OIII) brings out spectacular detail in the pillars.

description: One of the brightest emission nebulae. Swan-shaped structure visible in photos.
imaging_notes: Excellent H-alpha target. Low altitude from Northern Europe.

description: Sparse open cluster in a rich Milky Way field.
imaging_notes: Wide-field works best to include surrounding star field.

description: Distinctly oblate globular cluster, one of the most flattened known.
imaging_notes: Low altitude from Northern Europe.

description: Unusual combination of emission (red), reflection (blue) and dark nebulae. Divided by dust lanes.
imaging_notes: Broadband colour brings out the blue/red contrast. Pairs with M8 in same frame.

description: Compact open cluster near M20. Young cluster ~4.6 million years old.
imaging_notes: Often imaged alongside M20 and M8 in a single wide-field frame.

description: One of the nearest and brightest globular clusters. Surpassed in the northern sky only by M13.
imaging_notes: Impressive but low altitude from Northern Europe.

description: Rich open cluster with ~150 stars. Beautiful in wide-field images.
imaging_notes: Medium focal length to show individual stars and rich field.

description: Densest concentration of stars visible to the naked eye – a window through the Milky Way dust.
imaging_notes: Requires very wide field. Not a true object but a line-of-sight star cloud.

description: Large, scattered open cluster with a Cepheid variable (U Sgr).
imaging_notes: Wide-field setup to capture the full extent.

description: Compact open cluster with a less star-dense core region.
imaging_notes: Rich surrounding Milky Way field adds to the image.

description: Largest and brightest planetary nebula in the sky. First planetary nebula discovered by Messier.
imaging_notes: Excellent target at most focal lengths. OIII + H-alpha shows colour contrast.

description: Compact globular with a concentrated core, first found to contain a millisecond pulsar.
imaging_notes: Low altitude challenge from Northern Europe.

description: Small open cluster embedded in the rich Milky Way. Near the bright star Sadr.
imaging_notes: Include surrounding Cygnus star field for a dramatic image.

description: Compact globular with remarkable core-collapse – one of the densest central regions known.
imaging_notes: Challenging at low altitude from Northern Europe.

description: Nearest large spiral galaxy at 2.54 million light-years. Visible to the naked eye.
imaging_notes: Too large for a single frame at >600mm; mosaic needed for full galaxy. Core overexposes easily.

description: Compact dwarf elliptical satellite of M31. Often imaged alongside M31.
imaging_notes: Almost always captured in the same frame as M31.

description: Third-largest member of the Local Group. Very low surface brightness despite large angular size.
imaging_notes: Requires very dark skies. Short focal length mandatory for full extent. Long exposures.

description: Bright open cluster about ~1400 light-years away.
imaging_notes: Medium wide-field to capture the full cluster and surrounding field.

description: Rich open cluster with the fainter NGC 2158 visible in the background.
imaging_notes: Include NGC 2158 (background cluster) for depth. Medium focal length.

description: Open cluster in Auriga. One of three Messier clusters in the constellation.
imaging_notes: M36, M37 and M38 can be framed together with a wide field.

description: Richest of the three Auriga clusters. Contains ~150 stars.
imaging_notes: Most rewarding of the Auriga trio. Medium focal length to resolve individual stars.

description: Cross-shaped open cluster in Auriga. Smaller cluster NGC 1907 nearby.
imaging_notes: Include NGC 1907 in the frame for a nice double-cluster image.

description: Loose, bright open cluster. Very close to Earth at ~825 light-years.
imaging_notes: Very large – use wide field. Rich surrounding Cygnus star field.

description: Optical double star. Messier included it by mistake while searching for a nebula.
imaging_notes: Not a true deep sky object of interest. Rarely imaged intentionally.

description: Bright open cluster just south of Sirius. Contains a notable red giant at its centre.
imaging_notes: Use wide field to capture full extent and nearby Sirius region.

description: The most famous nebula in the sky. A stellar nursery 1344 light-years away.
imaging_notes: High dynamic range challenge – core overexposes easily. HDR stacking recommended.

description: Part of the Orion Nebula complex, separated by a dust lane.
imaging_notes: Almost always captured in the same frame as M42.

description: Large, bright open cluster visible to the naked eye. ~730 million years old.
imaging_notes: Very large – requires very short focal length or mosaic. Rich star colours.

description: The most famous star cluster. Surrounded by reflection nebulosity.
imaging_notes: Extremely large – wide-field essential. Blue reflection nebulae beautiful in long exposures.

description: Rich open cluster with embedded planetary nebula NGC 2438 (a superimposed foreground object).
imaging_notes: Include NGC 2438 for a unique open cluster + PN combination.

description: Bright, loose open cluster. Contrasts well with the rich M46 nearby.
imaging_notes: Image alongside M46 for a side-by-side cluster comparison.

description: Large, bright open cluster. Was 'lost' for nearly 200 years due to Messier's position error.
imaging_notes: Very large – wide field needed. Rich in yellow/orange giant stars.

description: Brightest member of the Virgo Cluster. Giant elliptical galaxy.
imaging_notes: Include companion VCC 1231 in the frame. Medium focal length.

description: Dense open cluster with a heart-shaped arrangement. Contains ~200 stars.
imaging_notes: Medium focal length to resolve individual stars.

description: Classic face-on spiral with interacting companion NGC 5195. Shows spiral structure in photos.
imaging_notes: Showpiece galaxy. Medium focal length captures both M51 and NGC 5195.

description: Rich, compact open cluster embedded in the Milky Way.
imaging_notes: Rich surrounding field. Near the Bubble Nebula NGC 7635.

description: One of the most remote globular clusters in the Milky Way at ~58,000 light-years.
imaging_notes: NGC 5053 is a faint companion cluster 1° away.

description: Belongs to the Sagittarius Dwarf Galaxy, the first extragalactic globular cluster catalogued.
imaging_notes: Low altitude from Northern Europe. Appears very compact.

description: Large, loose globular – one of the least concentrated. Very low density.
imaging_notes: Very low altitude from Europe. Requires exceptional southern horizon.

description: Small, compact globular cluster between Lyra and Cygnus.
imaging_notes: Nice companion target to M57 in the same constellation.

description: The most iconic planetary nebula. Perfect smoke-ring shape.
imaging_notes: Small – requires >1000mm focal length for detail. OIII enhances the shell.

description: Brightest barred spiral in the Virgo Cluster.
imaging_notes: Part of the Virgo Cluster. Include nearby galaxies in a wide frame.

description: Elliptical galaxy in the Virgo Cluster.
imaging_notes: Often imaged with M60 and NGC 4647 in the same frame.

description: One of the largest elliptical galaxies in the Virgo Cluster.
imaging_notes: NGC 4647 interacts visually. Nice pair target.

description: Face-on barred spiral with well-defined arms. Multiple supernovae observed.
imaging_notes: Excellent face-on spiral structure. Medium focal length.

description: Asymmetric globular with a highly condensed, off-centre core.
imaging_notes: Low altitude from Northern Europe. One of the most irregular-looking globulars.

description: Multi-arm spiral galaxy showing a sunflower-like pattern in deep images.
imaging_notes: Long exposures reveal complex spiral arm structure.

description: Remarkable dark dust band on one side of the nucleus. Counter-rotating inner disk.
imaging_notes: Medium focal length to reveal the dark 'eye' feature clearly.

description: Member of the Leo Triplet with M66 and NGC 3628. Tightly wound spiral.
imaging_notes: Image all three Leo Triplet galaxies together at short focal length.

description: Largest of the Leo Triplet. Distorted by interaction with NGC 3628.
imaging_notes: Brightest of the trio. Include M65 and NGC 3628 for full triplet.

description: One of the oldest open clusters (~3.2-5 billion years old). Contains solar analogue stars.
imaging_notes: Rich star field. Medium focal length to resolve individual stars.

description: Southern globular cluster in Hydra.
imaging_notes: Low altitude from Northern Europe. Well-resolved in good conditions.

description: Compact southern globular, one of the most metal-rich known.
imaging_notes: Very low altitude from Northern Europe.

description: Compact globular similar in appearance to M69.
imaging_notes: Very low altitude from Northern Europe. Near M69 in the sky.

description: Unusual sparse globular, once classified as a dense open cluster.
imaging_notes: Rich surrounding field. Medium focal length.

description: Small, faint globular cluster. One of the more challenging Messier globulars.
imaging_notes: Requires dark skies. Long exposures needed.

description: Y-shaped asterism of 4 stars. Now known not to be a physical association.
imaging_notes: Not a true DSO. Rarely imaged as a primary target.

description: Grand design face-on spiral. Among the most difficult Messier objects due to very low surface brightness.
imaging_notes: Requires very dark skies and many hours of exposure. Spectacular under good conditions.

description: One of the most remote and compact Messier globulars at ~67,500 light-years.
imaging_notes: Appears very small. Long focal length to resolve.

description: Faintest of Messier's objects. Bipolar planetary nebula resembling a miniature M27.
imaging_notes: Long exposures and OIII filter recommended. Small – needs long focal length.

description: Seyfert galaxy with an active galactic nucleus. Complex spiral structure.
imaging_notes: Medium focal length. Bright core makes it manageable.

description: Brightest reflection nebula in the sky. Part of the Orion Molecular Cloud complex.
imaging_notes: Blue reflection nebulosity. Include surrounding Orion complex for context.

description: Winter globular cluster, unusual as most globulars are summer objects.
imaging_notes: Near Orion/Lepus region. Nice contrast with winter nebulae.

description: One of the densest globular clusters. Had a notable nova outburst in 1860.
imaging_notes: Low altitude from Northern Europe. Compact bright core.

description: One of the brightest galaxies, high declination – circumpolar from mid-latitudes.
imaging_notes: Always image with M82 in the same frame for a classic pair.

description: Irregular starburst galaxy with spectacular red H-alpha outflows from supernovae activity.
imaging_notes: H-alpha filter reveals the dramatic red outflows. Pair with M81.

description: Barred spiral with well-defined arms. Very low altitude from Northern Europe.
imaging_notes: Excellent galaxy structure but very low from Europe. Best from southern latitudes.

description: Lenticular galaxy in the Virgo Cluster core. Part of Markarian's Chain.
imaging_notes: Image as part of Markarian's Chain with M86 and other Virgo galaxies.

description: Lenticular galaxy, northernmost Virgo Cluster member.
imaging_notes: Interacting companion NGC 4394 nearby.

description: Elliptical/lenticular galaxy near M84 in Markarian's Chain. Moving towards us.
imaging_notes: Part of the famous Markarian's Chain – wide field for best result.

description: Giant elliptical with a famous relativistic jet. Home of the first imaged black hole.
imaging_notes: The jet is only visible in very long exposures. Medium focal length.

description: Spiral galaxy in the Virgo Cluster showing dust lanes in long exposures.
imaging_notes: Medium focal length. Part of Virgo/Coma cluster region.

description: Nearly circular elliptical galaxy in the Virgo Cluster.
imaging_notes: Part of Virgo Cluster field.

description: One of the few galaxies blueshifted – moving towards us. Virgo Cluster member.
imaging_notes: Large Virgo galaxy. Medium focal length.

description: Barred spiral in Virgo Cluster. Was the 'missing Messier' for 200 years.
imaging_notes: Faint. Requires dark skies and substantial exposure time.

description: Second-brightest globular in Hercules after M13. Often overlooked.
imaging_notes: Image alongside M13 for comparison. Nearly as impressive.

description: Rich open cluster with a butterfly-like pattern of stars.
imaging_notes: Medium focal length. Lower altitude from Northern Europe.

description: Starburst spiral with an unusually bright, compact core. Two outer ring structures.
imaging_notes: Bright core contrasts with fainter outer rings. Medium focal length.

description: Barred spiral galaxy in the Leo I Group. Inner ring and bar structure visible.
imaging_notes: Part of Leo Group with M96 and M105. Wide field captures all three.

description: Brightest of the Leo I Group. Asymmetric structure from past interaction.
imaging_notes: Image with M95 and M105 for the Leo Group.

description: Large, faint planetary nebula with two dark spots resembling owl's eyes.
imaging_notes: OIII filter reveals the twin 'eye' cavities. Image alongside M108.

description: Nearly edge-on spiral galaxy in the Virgo Cluster.
imaging_notes: Elongated edge-on appearance. Part of Virgo Cluster region.

description: Nearly face-on spiral. One of the original 'grand design' spirals.
imaging_notes: Low surface brightness. Requires dark skies and long exposures.

description: Grand design face-on spiral, one of the brightest in Virgo Cluster.
imaging_notes: Well-defined spiral arms. Medium focal length works well.

description: Large face-on spiral with very low surface brightness. Asymmetric arm structure.
imaging_notes: Requires dark skies. Short focal length needed for full extent. Many hours of exposure.

description: Edge-on lenticular galaxy with a prominent dust lane. Identity disputed (possibly NGC 5866).
imaging_notes: Striking edge-on profile with dark dust lane. Medium focal length.

description: Compact open cluster near the W-pattern of Cassiopeia.
imaging_notes: Compact cluster in rich Milky Way field.

description: One of the most photogenic galaxies – prominent dark dust lane across a bright bulge.
imaging_notes: Iconic target. Medium focal length to show full galaxy with dust lane detail.

description: Bright elliptical galaxy, central member of the Leo I Group.
imaging_notes: Part of Leo Group with M95 and M96.

description: Active Seyfert spiral galaxy with anomalous spiral arms of hot gas.
imaging_notes: Large – medium focal length. H-alpha reveals anomalous gas arms.

description: Loose, sparsely populated globular in Ophiuchus.
imaging_notes: One of the more loosely structured Messier globulars.

description: Edge-on irregular spiral galaxy near M97.
imaging_notes: Image alongside M97 (Owl Nebula) for a classic galaxy + PN combination.

description: Barred spiral galaxy near Gamma Ursae Majoris in the bowl of the Big Dipper.
imaging_notes: Include nearby star Phecda for framing. Medium focal length.

description: Dwarf elliptical satellite of M31. Part of the Andromeda Group.
imaging_notes: Almost always in the same wide-field frame as M31.
